Name: neural_rate_distortion
Title: Exploring the Rate-Distortion Tradeoff in Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: There exists an optimal tradeoff between the compression rate of a neural network and the distortion of its predictive performance, which can be characterized using rate-distortion theory. We hypothesize that this tradeoff can be approximated using a variational information bottleneck approach, allowing for the derivation of achievable rates and distortion bounds for compressed neural networks.
Related Work: While there have been numerous efforts to compress neural networks, few have taken an information-theoretic approach to characterizing the fundamental limits of compression. Our work builds upon the concept of rate-distortion theory and the information bottleneck method, but applies these ideas to the specific context of neural network compression.
Abstract: This work explores the rate-distortion tradeoff in neural network compression, seeking to characterize the fundamental limits of compressing neural networks while preserving their predictive performance. We develop a variational information bottleneck approach to approximate the rate-distortion curve, allowing for the derivation of achievable rates and distortion bounds. Our framework provides a principled way to evaluate and compare different compression methods, and sheds light on the optimal tradeoff between compression rate and predictive performance.
Experiments: Derive achievable rates and distortion bounds for compressed neural networks using the variational information bottleneck approach
Evaluate the performance of different compression methods (e.g. pruning, quantization) in terms of their proximity to the rate-distortion curve
Investigate the effect of different distortion measures (e.g. mean squared error, cross-entropy) on the rate-distortion tradeoff
Risk Factors and Limitations: The accuracy of the variational approximation may be limited by the choice of variational distribution
The computational cost of evaluating the rate-distortion curve may be high for large neural networks
The results may not generalize to all types of neural networks or compression methods
